entries.text: hi guys my name is Anna and in this
video I'm going to be talking about
things to not do in Korea as a foreigner
I'm doing this video because some people
ask me what they should be mindful of
while they're visiting Korea and before
I start getting into the place let me
just preface by saying that none of this
is a hundred percent the Bible not every
single Korean gets bothered or things
this way so this is simply just my
advice but it's not absolute set rule in
Stern and there might not be any real
consequences from you not following
these things but just putting that out
there for those of you who may not know
I'm Korean I was born in Korea but I
lived in the UK for a long time and I've
also traveled a lot of the world so I've
seen how other countries are and I think
I can just give advice on things that
might be okay over there compared to how
things are in Korea so again not saying
I know everything but just giving you
guys advice so let's go so number one
full-on extreme public displays of
affection so things like making out or
like grouping each other's private parts
in public that is not okay like hugs are
putting your arms around each other and
like a quick Peck here and there that's
completely fine but if I look her to
Paris or even in London I see people
just full I'm almost going at it in
public that is not okay in Korea so
avoid really extreme public displays of
affection in a public space number two
shoes obviously in most Asian countries
you don't wear shoes in the house I
think a lot of people already know this
but also when I went to like New York
Subway I would see people put their feet
up on the chair in front of them on
trains and in Korea is really not okay
to do that that includes train seats or
park bench seats just put your feet on
the ground nowhere else basically that's
the general rule so a lot of Korean
buildings they actually have slippers
you take your shoes off and then for the
actual room they have slippers for
people and I've seen some foreigners
complain about this because they're like
I don't want to take off my shoes I
think my shoes look cute with this
outfit I don't really want to wear these
pink slippers or whatever but you have
to do it you just have to follow the
rules absolutely no shoes allowed in the
house and not on public seats number
three is is jaywalking and Crossing on a
red light even if there are absolutely
no cars around in Korea just follow the
traffic lights when I lived in London if
I go to New York I know everybody just
jaywalks if there are no cars around
everyone thinks it's fine that you just
cross the road whatever but in Korea
really don't do that I was even
surprised at this because I was with my
dad once and it was like really God
early time in the morning like five or
six a.m there were absolutely no cars
right there and we just had to cross the
road to get to the hospital it was the
red light Crossing and I was like but
Dad call me just walked there's like no
one here he was like no in Korea we
don't do that no no it's not okay
so we waited until the light turned
green even if no cars went by but yeah
that's just how Koreans do it just
follow the rules third jaywalk also
there was an incident a while ago where
two foreigners I think Americans they
were skateboarding down the hills of
etawan obviously as you go down you pick
up speed but there was a truck driver
coming from the other side and they both
crashed and luckily nobody got massively
injured from that incident but the
driver might have PTSD he thought he
might have killed someone so don't do
kind of stupid stuff like this be
respectful and use common sense because
that could have easily happened and
somebody really could have been injured
or like died so
yeah number four don't wait for the bill
to come to your table at the end of the
meal even I had to get used to this
again after I came back to Korea in
2020. in London you have your meal and
then the waitress she will bring your
bill and even the card machine to the
table and then you pay and then you
leave but the problem with that is
sometimes the waiter or the waitress
they could be waiting on everyone else
and you might not be able to pay for
like 20 minutes half an hour so in Korea
as soon as you're done with your meal
there's a counter that you go up to you
pay don't wait for the bill to come to
you there are some restaurants where
that might be an exception but I would
say most of the cases after you finish
you go you pay you leave I think this is
more efficient way of doing things so
yeah don't expect the bill to come to
you number five we're going to talk
about public transport because a lot of
you guys foreigners use it when you
travel to Seoul so don't sit on the
priority seats in the train and those
are when you go in through the carriage
doors there are like three and three
seats closest to the door and those are
strictly reserved for the elderly so do
not sit there and then you have like the
long run of seats there's a whole
general public and also pink seats are
reserved for pregnant ladies so don't
sit there unless you're pregnant talking
loudly or listening to music very loudly
and just drawing attention to yourself
on public transport is generally a No-No
people have usually their airpods or
they're watching stuff on their phone if
you're with a friend just talk on the
quieter side and just chill on the train
number six people ask me like is it
still the case that it's inappropriate
to show like chest cleavage in Korea I
would say especially in regards to
foreigners because I think people are
way more lenient with foreigners they're
not really going to care so much and
it's because it's summer everyone in
Korea kind of dresses in like short or
revealing clothing as well but you might
get stared at so if you feel
uncomfortable with that if you're
wearing like a low-cut top maybe bring
like a cardigan or like a jacket to
throw one for the train but like in the
summer on the streets there are loads of
people just wearing kind of what they
want and people are asking me is it okay
to show stomach I think it's fine
there's loads of people wearing crop
tops these days but like I said on
public transport when a lot of elderly
people are there as well maybe just
bring a light covering for the trade
there was one incident for me as well
when I was on the train with my friend
and I was wearing like a sidey crop top
a pair of Dickies trousers they were
kind of like trendy and like the top of
the trouser rolls over and it kind of
shows your belly button and like that
midriff area it's like the elderly lady
she like tapped me on my shoulder she's
like I don't think you did your trousers
up all the way she thought that I had
just forgot to like zip them up
completely to the top and I was like oh
no this is how you're supposed to wear
them it's fashion I think she's
genuinely concerned for me that I had
like left the house without remembering
so in instances like that it's basically
up to you but I don't think anyone is
going to really give you a hard time
about it but if you're just
uncomfortable with people staring at you
then that's just my advice obviously if
you're in Korea for a long term and you
actually like work here then in the
workplace then obviously be slightly
more professional and not wear too much
revealing clothing but this is kind of a
given in most countries I think so yeah
just keep that in mind number seven
again I preface this by saying Korean
could be way more lenient with
foreigners but in Korea if you're
handing something to someone it's polite
to give it with both hands instead of
just going like this and you also accept
with both hands so when you receive or
giving things just try to remember to do
with both party most Koreans will find
it like really Charming if you do that
because they won't really expect you to
know but they will find it Charming if
you do this so that's my advice number
eight I've seen some people that I know
do this when they come to Korea don't do
the Namaste gesture when you bow in
Korea because that is not a Korean
custom we don't do this that's used in
India and parts of Southeast Asia but in
Korea we just we just bow we don't do
this hand gesture so yeah that is not a
Korean custom so don't do that number
nine do not leave a tip at restaurants
it's not a thing here like in America
for example nobody thinks you're rude if
you don't leave a tip finish your meal
just pay what you owe they will give you
back change if you need change don't
leave a tip number 10 don't get offended
when people ask you for your age in the
west if somebody asks you like how old
are you that can sometimes come off as
quite rude but in Korea there is an age
hierarchy system and everyone just asks
each other for their age because
obviously Koreans we use honorifics and
how you address them and stuff so it is
important to figure out someone's age so
don't be offended if people ask you for
your age like kind of immediately after
asking for your name that's just a thing
in Korea number 11 don't expect like
Greetings or small talk with random
strangers because in Korea everyone
pretty much keeps to themselves and also
making like direct eye contact with
people on public transport it's not
really a thing like when I went to New
York last time people would just start
talking to me on the street hey how's it
going and just stuff like this in Korea
it's just really basically never happens
and also this is tied to number 12 but
if some people do come up to you and try
to make small talk be mindful that it
could be a cult trying to recruit you so
number 12 is don't join a cult because
like 100 of the time is not going to be
beneficial for you so if somebody
approaches you on the street and it's
like oh you can get like a free Fortune
reading or you can join this club where
you can make friends just ignore them
and just keep walking because yeah don't
join a cult and also don't try to hug
someone upon the first meeting Koreans
do not hug and create in a social
situation if a friend introduces you to
a new friend don't agree like hey what's
up and hug that is not a thing just here
and sometimes I do like handshake but
generally No hugs between Koreans when
you first meet someone okay and number
13 not saying everybody but some Koreans
especially like the elderly they can be
quite sensitive in regards to Japan
because Korea was under Japanese rule
from 1910 to 1945 so things like the
Rising Sun flag which is different from
the national flag of Japan the Rising
Sun flag that is actually an Imperial
War flag and sometimes you see that on
pieces of clothing or on bags or on
stickers so just be mindful of the fact
that some Koreans may find that
disrespectful because it is like a war
flag party nobody's gonna come up to you
and be like la la la but just something
to maybe just keep in mind because I
know a lot of westerners don't even know
about like the Rising Sun flag and the
meaning so this is just me letting you
know and lastly number 14 cat calling I
have personally never seen or
experienced men cat calling in South
Korea I've never seen it but I have
experienced it in London in America and
other Western countries obviously it's
just generally a rude thing to do even
back in those countries women don't like
it there but it's just simply not done
in South Korea so if you're a foreigner
if you're partying so and you just get
drunk and you just suddenly do cat
calling or just like saying stuff to
strangers just avoid doing that and
you'll be all good so those were my 15
points let me know if you guys have
anything you want to add or you want to
ask please like And subscribe and also
turn on the Bell notifications so you
don't miss out when I upload new videos
thank you for watching and I'll see you
in the next one bye
[Music]
